quality.rationale: 极化密度矩阵类比与多态叠加推导+GW探测物理场景+解答逻辑严密

origin.question: Problem 18.10. Define Stokes parameters for a plane gravitational wave and show how to calculate the fraction of circular polarization, linear polarization and the orientation of maximum linear polarization from the three Stokes parameters.
origin.answer: Fraction of radiation linearly polarized $= (\xi_1^2 + \xi_3^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}$Orientation of linear polarization: $\tan 4\psi = \xi_1 / \xi_3$Fraction of radiation circularly polarized $= |\xi_2|$ ($\xi_2$ positive for right circular polarization)Fraction of radiation polarized $= (\xi_1^2 + \xi_2^2 + \xi_3^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}$
origin.solution: Solution 18.10. A plane wave propagating in the $z$ -direction can be described at the position of a detector by$$h_{x x} = - h_{y y} = R e \left\{A_{+} e^{- i \omega t}\right\} \quad h_{x y} = h_{y x} = R e \left\{A_{\times} e^{- i \omega t}\right\}.$$If the plane wave is monochromatic (and hence $100\%$ polarized) $\mathbf{A}_{+}$ and $\mathbf{A}_{\times}$ will be constant. More generally if we are considering waves in a narrow bandwidth about $\omega$ , $\mathbf{A}_{+}$ and $\mathbf{A}_{\times}$ will be slow functions of time (i.e. slow compared to the wave oscillations). Since the intensity is proportional to the absolute squares of the A's we can define a polarization tensor$$\rho_{a b} \equiv \frac {\left. < A_{a} A_{b}^{*} >\right.}{\left| A_{+}\right|^{2} + \left| A_{\times}\right|^{2}}$$in analogy with the electromagnetic approach. [See for example Landau and Lifschitz, p. 122ff.] Following this analogy further we notice that this matrix is hermitian with a unit trace so we can write it as$$\rho_{\mathrm {a b}} = \frac {1}{2} \left[ \begin{array}{c c} 1 + \xi_{3} & \xi_{1} - \mathrm {i} \xi_{2} \\\vdots & \vdots \\\xi_{1} + \mathrm {i} \xi_{2} & 1 - \xi_{3} \end{array}\right].$$As in electromagnetism $\xi_1, \xi_2, \xi_3$ will be called the Stokes parameters of the wave. [Note: Some authors call these the normalized Stokes parameters.]These parameters characterize the polarization state of the wave. To get insight into the meaning of the parameters, let us consider a mixture of (1) unpolarized waves, (2) circularly polarized waves, and (3) linearly polarized waves with an orientation rotated about the $x$ and $y$ axis by an angle $\psi$ . Mathematically these three cases correspond to superpositions of(1) $\mathbf{A}_{+} = \mathbf{G}_{1}(\mathbf{t})$ , $\mathbf{A}_{\times} = \mathbf{G}_{2}(\mathbf{t})$ with $< |\mathbf{G}_{1}(\mathbf{t})|^2>= < |\mathbf{G}_{2}(\mathbf{t})|^2> \equiv < |\mathbf{G}|^2>$ and with $< \mathbf{G}_{1}(\mathbf{t})\mathbf{G}_{2}^{*}(\mathbf{t})> = 0$ (2) $\mathbf{A}_{+} = \mathbf{H}(t)$ $\mathbf{A}_{\times} = \pm \mathrm{iH}(t)$ (3) $\mathbf{A}_{+} = \mathbf{F}(t)\cos 2\psi$ $\mathbf{A}_{\times} = \mathbf{F}(t)\sin 2\psi$We assume of course that $\mathbf{G}_1, \mathbf{G}_2, \mathbf{F},$ and $\mathbf{H}$ are uncorrelated. The polarization matrix is then$$\begin{array}{l} \rho = \frac {1}{I} \left[ \begin{array}{l l} < | F |^{2} > \cos^{2} 2 \psi + < | G | >^{2} + < | H | >^{2} & < | F |^{2} > \sin 2 \psi \cos 2 \psi \mp i < | H |^{2} > \\\vdots & \vdots \\\xi_{1} + \mathrm {i} \xi_{2} & 1 - \xi_{3} \end{array}\right] \\ \mathbf {I} \equiv < | \mathbf {F} |^{2} > + 2 < | \mathbf {G} |^{2} > + 2 < | \mathbf {H} |^{2} > \\ \end{array}$$so that$$\xi_{3} = \frac {1}{I} < | \mathrm {F} |^{2} > \cos 4 \psi \qquad \xi_{1} = \frac {1}{I} < | \mathrm {F} |^{2} > \sin 4 \psi \qquad \xi_{2} = \pm \frac {2}{I} < | \mathrm {H} |^{2} >.$$We see finally that the polarization properties of the radiation can be found from the Stokes parameters in a manner very similar to that of electromagnetism.Fraction of radiation linearly polarized $= (\xi_1^2 + \xi_3^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}$Orientation of linear polarization: $\tan 4\psi = \xi_1 / \xi_3$Fraction of radiation circularly polarized $= |\xi_2|$ ($\xi_2$ positive for right circular polarization)Fraction of radiation polarized $= (\xi_1^2 + \xi_2^2 + \xi_3^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}$.
